text: The surgeon performs lysis of adhesions, carefully dissecting around the small bowel beneath the right-sided colostomy. The adhesions are cleared from the left side wall and pelvis, facilitating safe surgical access.
The surgeon carefully clears small bowel adhesions from the area surrounding the colostomy in the right lower quadrant, creating space for port placement.

The surgeon switches to a left-sided dock and begins a retrorectus dissection, starting just to the right of the midline. The peritoneum and preperitoneal fat are being taken down, allowing entry into the retrorectus space on the left side.

The surgeon separates the left posterior rectus sheath from the linea alba, moving into the pelvic space of Retzius. The dissection crosses over to the right side of the midline, revealing both rectus muscles. The posterior sheath dissection is extended towards the subxiphoid area, where the surgeon's left arm is positioned.

The surgeon identifies the semilunar line and carefully preserves a visible neurovascular bundle. The posterior elements in the peritoneum are preserved as the tissue eventrates into the hernia defect, although the procedure becomes challenging.

The surgeon performs a transversus abdominis release (TAR) on the left side, staying in the pre-transversalis plane to dissect inferiorly and posteriorly towards the hernia defect. A sponge is placed to manage minor bleeding as the dissection progresses behind the defect.

The surgeon navigates the pre-transversalis plane, moving down towards the superior aspect of the hernia defect. The left-sided trocar hole from the initial upper doc is visible, while the right arm is positioned below the colostomy in the lower right quadrant.

The surgeon continues dissection on the superior aspect of the hernia defect, revealing a scarred area. The dissected plane is visible as the surgeon moves behind the hernia defect, extending laterally during the TAR procedure.

The surgeon transects the hernia sac, noting the left epigastric artery positioned on the medial border of the hernia. The colostomy is visible on the right side, and efforts are made to preserve the artery during the procedure.

The surgeon continues to dissect the hernia sac and peritoneum from the perimeter of the hernia defect, re-entering the pre-transversalis plane lateral to the hernia. The dissection progresses laterally, ensuring the bowel is safely positioned away from the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the epigastric vessels near the indirect lipoma ligament and continues dissection through the fat layer down to the peritoneum, using a sponge to manage bleeding. The dissection progresses into the preperitoneal space, focusing on the myopectineal orifice on the left-hand side.

The surgeon identifies the hernia defect located near the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) and prepares to position the mesh for inferior coverage. The bladder is observed to be adhered to the pubic bone, likely due to previous surgical complications, but is carefully dissected free.

The surgeon achieves a clear view of the left myopectineal orifice after completing a superior to inferior transversus abdominis release (TAR) on the left side. The hernia defect is measured at thirteen by eleven centimeters.

The surgeon reduces the pressure to six and begins closing the hernia defect using a zero V-lock suture, invocating the pseudosac. The suture is carefully pulled through to cinch it down, ensuring not to penetrate the skin. The closure starts from the top and then proceeds from the bottom, with the stitch placed around the epigastric artery to secure the medial edge of the hernia wall.

The surgeon carefully stitches around the epigastric vessels to close the hernia defect, transitioning to direct stitching as the vessels move away from the defect's edge. The defect visibly comes together as the surgeon alternates stitches from above and below.

The surgeon ties two eighteen-inch sutures together and runs them in opposite directions, adding additional stitches. The surgical space is measured, revealing dimensions of approximately thirty-five centimeters from north to south and twenty centimeters from east to west.

The surgeon places a biosynthetic mesh over the primary hernia defect, measuring 25 centimeters from north to south and 18 centimeters from east to west. A piece of lightweight mesh is used to cover the myopectineal orifice, ensuring coverage into the pelvis.

The surgeon uses 2-0 silk sutures to fixate the mesh at multiple locations: below the colostomy, adjoining the inguinal hernia mesh and synthetic mesh to the abdominal wall at the inferior border, and at the lateral and medial borders.

The surgeon closes the posterior defect in the peritoneum using laparoscopy and an absorbable tacking device to secure the flap back in place, effectively covering the mesh. The flap shows good redundancy and is positioned without complications.
